content.full_text:
Re
Fern Polrrrcal PnacrrcEs CouvussroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
luJy 2,20A4
Peter M. Thorson
fuchards Watson Gershon
355 South Grand Avenue,40ft Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-3101
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-04-106
Dear Mr. Thorson:
This letter is in response to your request on behalfMayor Michael Naggar for
informal assistance regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act').r Nothing in this letter should be construid to evaluate any conduct which has
already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission') does not act as the finder of fact. (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Would Mayor Naggar be required to abstain from participation in a city council
matter involving a developer for whom he has provided land use consultant services, as a
contract employee or subcontractor of an engineering/planning firm, working on the
developer's project in another city?
CONCLUSION
MayorNaggar would have a conflict of interest underthe Act and would be
precluded from participating in a council matter involving a dweloper, upon whose
project he has provided land use consultant services as a contract employee or
subcontractor of an engineering/planning firm working on the developer's project, if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his employer.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
.r-g!i


File No. I-04-106
Page No. 2
FACTS
You have requested advice on behalf of Mayor Michael Naggm of the City of
Temecula. MayorNaggar has entered into an agreement with Odlg an engineering firm
(the'?irm), who has retained him to work as a development conzultant to assist the Firm
in obtaining land use entitlements for various projects. The Firm provides engineering
and limited planning services to a number of large developers who build single family
and multi-family housing in Southern Califomia. Mayor Naggm's contract provides for
payment on an hourly basis for work performed for the Firm. Some of the developers the
Firm represents may have projects within the City of Temecula You have indicated that
Mayor Naggar has no business or ownership interest in the Firm, and the developers who
employ the Firm are not a source of income to Mayor Naggar.
MayorNaggar is a real estate development consultant who has worked for a good
part of his private professional career in real estate asset managernent and development.
He has a recent history of engaging in real estate development and land use entitlement
work. Under the above agreement, Mayor Naggar has performed work as a land use
consultant for the Firm on a developer's project in another city.
You have stated that, based upon his experiencg Mayor Naggar can provide
valuable services to the Firm in its work on single-family and multi-familyhousing
projects, that he is qualified for this work and, based on these qualifications, the Firm has
a need to utilize his services.
Your letter acknowledges that the Firm is a source of income to MayorNa13fr,
and that he would need to abstain from participating in any decisions regarding
applications for land use entitlements in which the Firm is representing an applicant.
You have neither provided the name of the developer to whom this request
pertains, nor the nature of the specific governmental decision involved. Thereforq we
can only provide you with informal assistance regarding the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions.
ANALYSIS
Pornnrr,q,L Coxrr,rc'r or INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will 'lerform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have 
them." (Section sl00l&).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making participating in making; or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests.


File No. I-04-106
Page No. 3
Srrps I & 2: Is Mevon Naccan A PuBLrc Orrrcrar, Mar<rxc, PARTTcIrATTNG rN
MAKING, oR INFLUENCING A GovrRxuENTAL Drcrsrcxr?
As the mayor of the City of Temecula, Mayor Naggar is a public official under the
Act (section 82048). Consequently, he may not makg participate in making or otherwise
use his official position to influence any decision which will have a.ea"orubly foreseeable
material financial effect on any economic interest hemayhave.
Sincg as a member of the Temecula City Council, Mayor Naggar will be called
upon to consider whether the city should approve or disapprove any planning applications
or land use entitlernents, he would be making participating in making or otherrrise using
his of;Ecial position to influence a govenrmental decision.
srrr 3: rlons M,+.von NIGGAR rr,c,\ru a porruxrrar,r,y Drsguu.rrvnvcEcoNoMrc
Ixrrnssr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on thepublic generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investnent of $2,000 ormore (g s7103(a); reg. lg703.l(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partrer, trustee, employeg orholds anyposition
of managernent ($ 87103(d); reg.l8703.l(b));
O
o
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more ($ 871030);reg. LSTI3.Z);
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofincome, including
promised incomq which aggregates to $500 ormorelvithin 12 monthspriorto the
decision ($ 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);
A public of;ficial has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months pnor to the decision (g 87103(e);
reg.l87A3.4);
a
a
You have acknowledged that Mayor Naggar has an economic interest in his
source of income with the Firm.2 Your request for advice concerns any potential conflict
a
A public official has an economic interest in his or herpersonal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
($ 87103; reg. 18703.5).
2 As discussed herein, Mayor Naggar would have a source of income economic interest in the
Firm only if he receives $500 or more in income during the 12 monthperiod before the decision is made.


File No. I-04-106
Page No. 4
of interest in decisions involving the developer, because of Mayor Naggar's employment
by the Firm, working on the developer's project in another city.
Source of fncome: Section 87103 (c) states that apublic official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any source of income to the official. Regulation 18703.3 states that
"...a public official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received incomg including commission income and incentive comp€nsation ...
aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) within 12 months prior to the time when the
relevant govenrmental decision is made."
Under the facts you have presented, Mayor Naggar is an hourly employee of the
Firm. Therefore, if Mayor Naggarreceived income of $500 ormore during the last 12
months, he would have a source of income economic interest in theFirm. You also
indicated in our telephone conversation that Mayor Naggar has no ownership interest in
the Firm, and therefore would not have an economic interest in the business entity.
Accordingly, since the developer employed the Firm, and Mayor Naggarhad no
ownership interest in the Firm, and MayorNaggar's source of income was provid"d by
the Firm for services rendered to the Firm, absent any other factors,3 he would have no
direct source of income economic interest involving the developer. However, he must
consider potential disqualification arising from his economic interest in the Firm.
Srrp 4: Is Tm Ecoxourc Ixrnnnsr Ilrnncrr,y oR INDrRrcrLy lrwor,yrn rN THE
GorrrnmuENTAL Drcrsron ?
In order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is direcfly involved or indirectly involved in the
govemmental decision. (Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions which affect
sources of incomq the standards set forth in regulation lg70/,.l apply.
Regulation I 87 O4.l (a) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources ofincomg and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a desision before an
official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates t}reproceoding in which the decision will bemadeby
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similarrequest or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning
the decision before the official or the official's agency. Aperson is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuancg renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
3One factor that may need to be considered is whether the developer is an'btherwise related
business entity" as set forth under regulation 18703.1(d)(2) (copy enclosed).


File No. I-04-106
Page No. 5
Accordingly, unless the Firm meets the criteria set forth abovg it would be
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Srpp 5: Marnnrar,ITY STAI{DARD
Under regulation 18705.3(b)(1), the materiality standards for indirectly involved
sources of income which me business entities are set forth in regulation 13705.1(c) (copy
encloseO and depend on the relative size of the entity. Generally, the financial effects of
a goveflrmental decision me deemed not to be material unless the decision impacts the
Firm, as set forth in the standards listed theretmder. You have not presented us with
enough facts to analyze this further.
Srnp 6: RrasoxlrlY FoRESEEABLE
Once the applicable materially standard has been applied, the next step is to
determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect ofthe governme,ntal
decision will meet that materiality standmd for the economic interest involved. The
effect of a decision is considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur (regulation 18706(a)) and it would need to be determined by
the facts involved in that governmental decision. If it is not reasonably foreseeable that
there will be a material financial effect on the firm, he or she does not have a conflict of
interest within the meaning of the Act.
Smps 7,lxo 8: Pusr,rc GnNsRALr.y & Lncnr,r,y Rrqulnro pa,nrrcrp.lrrox
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the'lublic generally''or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable herein.
If you have any other questions regarding this mattetr, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
Drt
fu,kL
(5',
By: William J. Lexlkeit
Counsel, Legal Divisi on
Enclosures
wJLjg

matter involving a developer for whom he has provided land use consultant services, as a
contract employee or subcontractor of an engineering/planning firm, working on the
developer's project in another city?

CONCLUSION: MayorNaggar would have a conflict of interest underthe Act and would be
precluded from participating in a council matter involving a dweloper, upon whose
project he has provided land use consultant services as a contract employee or
subcontractor of an engineering/planning firm working on the developer's project, if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his employer.
